chunks.text: Berkeley is one of the cradles of modern theoretical computer science. Over the last thirty years, our graduate students and, sometimes, their advisors have done foundational work on NP-completeness, cryptography, derandomization, probabilistically checkable proofs, quantum computing, and algorithmic game theory. In addition, Berkeley's Simons Institute for the Theory of Computing regularly brings together theory-oriented researchers from all over the world to collaboratively work on hard problems. The institute organizes a sequence of programs based on topics, supported by workshops and other events.
chunks.question: What are some key areas of foundational work in theoretical computer science at Berkeley?
chunks.answer: NP-completeness, cryptography, quantum computing, algorithmic game theory.

chunks.text: Using computation as a lens to the sciences: Like probabilistic thinking in the last century, computational thinking will give mathematics and, more generally, science a new language to use and the ability to formulate new fundamental questions.
chunks.question: How does computational thinking impact science and mathematics?
chunks.answer: It provides a new language and enables new fundamental questions.

chunks.text: Applications of theoretical computer science: We are studying the applications of theoretical computer science in many sciences, including economics (with our work on computational game theory and mechanism design), physics (with our work on random structures and quantum computing), biology, and pure mathematics (especially geometry, functional analysis, and additive number theory).
chunks.question: In which fields are the applications of theoretical computer science studied?
chunks.answer: Economics, physics, biology, and pure mathematics.

chunks.text: Foundational questions in cryptography: We study foundational questions on subjects such as computing on encrypted data, functional encryption, program obfuscation, verifiable computation, zero-knowledge proofs, and others. We also investigate concrete efficiency aspects and implementations of cryptographic protocols.
chunks.question: What topics are studied in foundational cryptography research?
chunks.answer: Encrypted data computing, functional encryption, zero-knowledge proofs.

chunks.text: Research Centers: Berkeley Laboratory for Information and System Sciences, Berkeley Quantum Information and Computation Center, Center for the Theoretical Foundations of Learning, Inference, Information, Intelligence, Mathematics and Microeconomics at Berkeley, Simons Institute for the Theory of Computing.
chunks.question: What are some research centers related to theory at Berkeley?
chunks.answer: Simons Institute, Berkeley Quantum Information and Computation Center.

chunks.question: Which faculty members have received the ACM A.M. Turing Award?
chunks.answer: Shafi Goldwasser and Richard M. Karp.

chunks.text: Related Courses: CS 170. Efficient Algorithms and Intractable Problems, CS 172. Computability and Complexity, CS 174. Combinatorics and Discrete Probability, CS 194. Special Topics, CS 270. Combinatorial Algorithms and Data Structures, CS 271. Randomness and Computation, CS 273. Foundations of Parallel Computation, CS 274. Computational Geometry, CS 276. Cryptography, CS 278. Machine-Based Complexity Theory.
chunks.question: What are some related courses in theoretical computer science?
